_comment: DRAFT -- Chapter 1 (Alphabet), reconstructed from 1_ALPHAB.TBK strings + CHAPT_1 audio inventory. Fields marked _verify need checking against the running original in DOSBox. Menu structure and instruction texts are extracted verbatim. Audio IDs follow the naming contract.
title: The Greek Alphabet
objectives: say the names for each of the letters of the Greek alphabet
know the sound each of the letters makes
recognize the special characteristics of some letters
identify the vowels and know which ones are short and which ones are long
gain an appreciation for the history of the Greek language
translate and write 10 vocabulary words in Greek

alphabet._comment: Shared letter table. audioFull = A_<letter> (~4s, name & sound). audioShort = A_<letter>N (~1s, name only; listen-verified). Chart/exercise taps and Pronounce/Check Answer use audioShort; the Learn Letters stepper and the Names & Sounds / Capitals drills use audioFull. A_NAME_1..24 are PERSONAL names (readingLists), not letter audio.

vowels.note: See Learn Vowels note (iota types, alpha, upsilon).

alphabet.diphthongDefinition: A Diphthong is a combination of two vowels that makes only one sound. The second vowel is either ι or υ.
alphabet.iotaSubscriptDefinition: An Iota Subscript is a diphthong which is formed when an iota follows a long vowel. The iota is written under the vowel (subscript). The pronunciation is the same as the normal vowel. The transliteration is what you would expect from a normal diphthong (ai, ei, and oi).
alphabet._verify_soundHints: capture chart rows Theta..Omega for remaining soundHint values

learn.title: Learn Letters
learn.instructions: Click on letter to hear its name & sound

items.label: Final Sigma
items.text: when sigma is the last letter of a word the form ς is used instead of the normal σ.
items.note: NOTE: for this chapter ignore the accent and breathing marks which are seen above the letters.

items.label: Nasal Gamma
items.text: when gamma (γ) is followed by γ, κ, χ, or ξ it is pronounced as "n" instead of the normal "g."

items.label: Letter Confusion
items.text: Be careful not to confuse the following letters

items.label: Zeta Pronunciation
items.text: ζ is pronounced "dz" in some grammars (Machen, Wenham) unless it is the first letter of the word in which case it is pronounced as a "z". Greek Tutor will follow this convention. Others always pronounce it as a "z" (cf. Mounce).

items.label: Pronunciation variations
items.text:
There are variations on the pronunciation of several letters. Some will pronounce π as "pie" others attempting to be more consistent will pronounce it "pee". Omicron also has several variations: 1) as the "o" in "hope" which is the same as the omega, 2) as the "o" in "Boston" ("aw" like sound), or 3) as the "o" in "not", in which case it sounds like the alpha. We have adopted the third choice here. It is also particularly difficult to know when the iota is long or short. Some will make the upsilon sound like the German ü. The chi is pronounced by many like the "ch" in the Gaelic word "loch" but many also pronounce it like the "ch" in "chemical" which makes it sound like a "k". Phi, Chi, Psi some will pronounce with an English long "i" rather than as a long Greek iota which is pronounced "phee" as in this program. Modern Greek will pronounce many of the letters very differently.

The point is there is variation in how the letters are pronounced and the student should go with whatever method their teacher suggests. It is a dead language and to get lost on the fine points of phonetics is counterproductive.

sixPointsContent.text: Machen, p. 9; Wenham, pp. 18f.; Mounce, pp. 8f.; Summers/Sawyer, p. 5

items.text: for the first word in a quotation (there are no quotation marks in Greek); and
for the first word in a paragraph.

content.text: Early Greek manuscripts, called Uncials, were written totally in capital letters with no spaces between the words. Later Greek manuscripts were written in minuscules (lower case letters) with breaks between the words. Modern texts are written in minuscules.

learn.title: Learn Vowels
learn._verify: whether vowel letters are clickable for audio

content.preamble: Iota is the most versatile letter. There are three types of iota:

items.text: It may be short sounding like the "i" in "hit".
It may be long sounding like the "i" in "machine".
It may be a consonant when found in initial positions of proper names where it sounds like a "y" in "yes".

content.text: Alpha sounds the same whether long or short.
Upsilon sometimes becomes a "y" when brought over into English words.
Machen, p. 10; Mounce, pp. 9f.; Wenham, p. 19

learn.title: Learn Diphthongs
learn.note: A Diphthong is a combination of two vowels that makes only one sound. The second vowel is either ι or υ.
learn._verify: confirmed via critique: tiles play diphthong sound; example words play example audio

content.text: Diphthongs are generally treated as long vowels. However, when either οι or αι occur at the end of the word, though they are pronounced the same, they are treated as short vowels for accent purposes.

learn.title: Learn Iota Subscripts
learn.note: An Iota Subscript is a diphthong which is formed when an iota follows a long vowel. The iota is written under the vowel (subscript). The pronunciation is the same as the normal vowel. The transliteration is what you would expect from a normal diphthong (ai, ei, and oi).

content.text: Greek is one of the oldest members of the Indo-European family of languages. Other members of this family are Sanskrit, which is older, and Latin and its descendants (the Romance languages: French, Spanish, etc.), which are younger. English is derived from the Teutonic branch and Russian from the Slavic branch of the Indo-European family. Hebrew is found in a totally different family--Semitic languages (Aramaic, Akkadian, Ugaritic etc.).
The Greek language has developed through five stages:

items.label: Formative Period
items.text: This period extended from "Linear B" (ca 1200 BC) down through the time of Homer (ca 900 BC).

items.label: Classical Period
items.text: The Classical Period was from the time of Homer down to Alexander the Great (330 BC). There were three dialects during this period (Doric, Aeolic, and Ionic). Attic, a branch of Ionic, became the predominant form in which most of the famous classical Greek authors wrote, such as Plato and Aristotle.

items.label: The Koine Period
items.text: As Alexander spread the Greek language and culture through the ancient world many of the subtleties of classical Greek were lost. Greek was simplified and changed as it interfaced with other cultures. This common language Greek came to be known as Koine (common) Greek. It was the language used from 300 BC to 300 AD. It was in this language that the Septuagint (the Greek translation of Old Testament), the New Testament and the writings of the early Church fathers were written. The nature of Koine eluded scholars because of its simplicity when compared to Classical Greek, which led some scholars to explain it as a "Holy Ghost" language created just for the Bible. After the Egyptian papyri, inscriptions, and ostraca were found, scholars realized that the New Testament was written in the common everyday language of the people of those times. At points the New Testament will manifest Hebraisms where the influence of Hebrew and/or Aramaic may be seen.

items.label: The Byzantine Period
items.text: During the Byzantine Period (A.D. 330-1453), Greek was spoken in the eastern half of the Roman empire which was centered in Constantinople.

items.label: The Modern Period
items.text: The Modern Period dates from 1453 to the present. Modern Greek is closer to Koine than it is to Classical Greek.

learn.title: Learn Vocabulary
learn.note: Ignore the accents and markings over letters for now. These will be learned in the next chapter.

items: Kubo, Sakae. A Beginner's New Testament Greek Grammar (Washington D.C.: University Press of America, 1979).
LaSor, William S. Handbook of New Testament Greek (Grand Rapids: Eerdmans, 1973).
Machen, J. Gresham. New Testament Greek for Beginners (Toronto: The Macmillan Company, 1923).
* Mounce, William D. Basics of Biblical Greek: Grammar (Grand Rapids: Zondervan, 1993).
Story, J. Lyle and Cullen Story. Greek to Me: Learning New Testament Greek Through Memory Visualization (San Francisco: Harper & Row, 1979).
Summers, Ray and Thomas Sawyer. Essentials of New Testament Greek (Nashville: Broadman & Holman, 1995).
Wenham, J. W. The Elements of New Testament Greek (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1965).
* Aland, B., et al., eds. The Greek New Testament, 4th ed. (Stuttgart: United Bible Society, 1993).
Black, David A. Using New Testament Greek in Ministry (Grand Rapids: Baker Book House, 1993).
Blass, F. and Debrunner, A. trans. R.W. Funk. A Greek Grammar of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature (Chicago: University Press, 1961).
Dana, H.E. and Julius Mantey. A Manual Grammar of the Greek New Testament (Toronto: The Macmillan Company, 1927).
Fanning, Buist M. Verbal Aspect in New Testament Greek (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1990).
Grassmick, John D. Principles and Practice of Greek Exegesis (Dallas: Dallas Theological Seminary, 1974).
Greenlee, J. Harold. A Concise Exegetical Grammar of New Testament Greek (Grand Rapids: Eerdmans, 1953).
Levinsohn, Stephen. Discourse Features of New Testament Greek (Dallas: Summer Institute of Linguistics, 1992).
Louw, J. P. Semantics of New Testament Greek (Philadelphia: Fortress Press, 1982).
Mouton, J., W. F. Howard, and N. Turner. A Grammar of New Testament Greek, 4 vols. (Edinburgh: T. & T. Clark, 1963).
Perschbacher, Wesley. New Testament Greek Syntax (Chicago: Moody Press, 1995).
Porter, S.E. Idioms of the Greek New Testament (Sheffield: JSOT, 1992).
Robertson, A.T. A Grammar of the Greek New Testament in Light of Historical Research (Nashville: Broadman, 1923).
Robertson, A.T. and W. Hersey Davis. A New Short Grammar of the Greek Testament (Grand Rapids: Baker Book House, 1931).
*Wallace, Daniel. An Exegetical Syntax of the Greek New Testament (Grand Rapids: Zondervan, 1995).
Zerwick, Maximilian. Biblical Greek (Rome: Scripta Pontificii Instituti Biblici, 1963).
* Bauer, W., Arndt, W., Gingrich, F. and Danker, F. eds. A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature (Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1979).
Liddell, Henry G. and Robert Scott. A Greek-English Lexicon (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1925).
Newman, B. M. A Concise Greek-English Dictionary of the New Testament (London: United Bible Societies, 1971).
Louw, Johannes and Nida, Eugene. Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament Based on Semantic Domains (New York: United Bible Societies, 1988).
* Metzger, Bruce M. Lexical Aids for Students of New Testament Greek (Princeton: Theological Book Agency, 1972).
Morrison, Clinton and David Barnes. New Testament Word Lists for Rapid Reading of the Greek New Testament (Grand Rapids: Wm. B. Eerdmans, 1966).
Trenchard, Warren. The Student's Complete Vocabulary Guide to the Greek New Testament (Grand Rapids: Zondervan Publishing House, 1992).
Van Voorst, Robert. Building Your New Testament Greek Vocabulary (Grand Rapids: Wm. B. Eerdmans, 1990).
Alsop, John R. Index to the Bauer-Arndt-Gingrich Greek Lexicon (Grand Rapids: Zondervan, 1968).
Douglas, J.D. ed., The New Greek-English Interlinear New Testament (Wheaton: Tyndale, 1990).
* Kubo, Sakae. A Reader's Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and a Beginner's Guide for the Translation of New Testament Greek (Berrien Springs: Andrews University Press, 1975).
Mounce, W. D. Analytical Lexicon of the Greek New Testament (Grand Rapids: Zondervan, 1992).
Perschbacher, W. J. Refresh Your Greek: Practical Helps for Reading the Greek New Testament (Chicago: Moody Press, 1989).
Rieneker, F. A Linguistic Key to the Greek New Testament (Grand Rapids: Zondervan, 1980).
Aland, K. and B. Aland. The Text of the New Testament (Grand Rapids: Eerdmans, 1989).
Greenlee, J.H. Introduction to New Testament Textual Criticism (Grand Rapids: Eerdmans, 1964).
Metzger, B. M. The Text of the New Testament: Its Transmission, Corruption, and Restoration (NY: Oxford, 1992).
Balz, H. and G. Schneider. eds. Exegetical Dictionary of the New Testament (3 vols.) (Grand Rapids: Eerdmans, 1993).
Brown, Collin, ed. The New International Dictionary of New Testament Theology (3 vols.) (Grand Rapids: Zondervan, 1975).
Kittel, G. The Theological Dictionary of the New Testament. Trans. G. Bromiley. (9 vols.) (Grand Rapids: Eerdmans, 1979). Also available in a one volume condensed version.
